framework.reasoning: The paper explicitly bases its implementation on the CleanRL PPO codebase (Huang et al., 2022, https://github.com/vwxyzjn/cleanrl), which is PyTorch-based. The models are small feed-forward MLPs and the bottleneck is the pure-Python/CPU limit order book simulator (128 parallel CPU environments), so CUDA is optional/recommended-but-not-required. Config needs are modest (flat hyperparameter sets per market/algorithm), so plain YAML is sufficient and keeps the repo dependency-light rather than pulling in Hydra/OmegaConf.

tensor_flows.name: LN policy forward + sample
steps: state: [B, state_dim] <- input (market + private normalized features, Appendix B.3)
h1 = tanh(Linear(state))  -> [B, 128]
h2 = tanh(Linear(h1))     -> [B, 128]
mu = Linear(h2)           -> [B, K]  (K=6, output layer bias init to -1, weight gain 1e-5)
x ~ Normal(mu, Sigma) where Sigma = sigma_i * I_K (variance from Eq. 12 schedule)  -> [B, K]
a = LogisticNormalTransform.forward(x)  -> [B, K+1]  (Eq. in Section 4.2)
return a, x, mu

tensor_flows.name: Value network forward
steps: state: [B, state_dim] <- input
h1 = tanh(Linear(state)) -> [B, 128]
h2 = tanh(Linear(h1))    -> [B, 128]
v = Linear(h2)           -> [B, 1]
return v

tensor_flows.name: One environment step (execution_env)
steps: action a=(a_0,...,a_K) <- input, a_0*M(t) rounded to market order size, a_1..a_{K-1}*M(t) rounded sequentially to limit order sizes at ticks 1..K-1 above best bid (Section 3.2)
cancel existing algorithm limit orders as needed, worst queue position first, to reach new allocation
submit market order of size floor(a_0*M(t)) -> immediate cash flow via LimitOrderBook.submit_market_order
submit/adjust limit orders at levels 1..K-1 via LimitOrderBook.submit_limit_order
advance simulation clock by dt=15s: run NoiseTraders.step, TacticalTraders.step, StrategicTrader.step (if present) at their own Poisson/periodic rates within (t, t+dt]
accumulate cash flow r_bar(s_n,a_n) from all market/limit fills of the algorithm's orders in (t, t+dt]
if n == N-1 and inventory remains: force market order of size M(t_N), add to cash flow
normalize reward via r = (r_bar - gamma_n * p_b(0)) / M  (Section 3.3)
compute next normalized state vector via FeatureNormalizer.normalize_state (Appendix B.3)
return next_state, reward, done=(n==N-1), info

tensor_flows.name: Training iteration (Algorithm 1)
steps: collect tau=1280 trajectories of length N=10 from 128 parallel envs (10 trajectories per env) using current stochastic policy phi_theta_i
for each (s_n,k, a_n,k) compute advantage A = sum_{l=n}^{N-1} r(s_l,k,a_l,k) - V_vartheta_i(s_n,k)  (Eq. 13)
policy loss = -(1/(tau*N)) * sum A * log phi_theta(x_n,k | s_n,k)  (Eq. 14); backprop, Adam step lr=5e-4
value loss = (1/(tau*N)) * sum (V_vartheta(s_n,k) - sum_l r(s_l,k,a_l,k))^2  (Eq. 15); backprop, Adam step lr=5e-4
scale down covariance Sigma = sigma_{i+1} * I_K per Eq. 12
repeat for H=400 iterations

model.K: 6  # simplex dim: market order + 5 limit levels + no-order action (confidence 0.95)

training.adam_beta1: 0.9  # ASSUMED: PyTorch default, not stated in paper (confidence 0.6)
training.adam_beta2: 0.999  # ASSUMED: PyTorch default, not stated in paper (confidence 0.6)

evaluation.eval_seed: 1  # ASSUMED: different seed than training, exact value not stated (confidence 0.4)

hardware.device: cpu  # simulator is CPU-bound; policy/value nets can use cuda if available

Reward histograms, inventory curves, training curves (Figures 3-13)

Number of Monte Carlo evaluation simulations

risk_assessment.description: Exact integer-rounding procedure for converting continuous simplex allocations to lot counts is under-specified (SIR ambiguity, confidence 0.6).
risk_assessment.mitigation: Implement a configurable RoundingStrategy in execution_env.py with the paper's described 'sequential floor' as default; expose alternative largest-remainder strategy behind a config flag and document the assumption clearly in code comments.

risk_assessment.description: Value network initialization gains not 100% explicitly re-confirmed as identical to policy network (SIR ambiguity, confidence 0.65).
risk_assessment.mitigation: Use orthogonal init with gain=0.01 for all value network layers (matching policy network's non-final layers) and flag this with a `# ASSUMED` comment in models/value.py.

risk_assessment.description: Adam optimizer beta1/beta2 not stated in paper (confidence 0.6).
risk_assessment.mitigation: Use PyTorch defaults (0.9, 0.999) and expose as config-overridable hyperparameters in config.yaml.

risk_assessment.description: The limit order book simulator with FIFO queues, Poisson-process traders, and imbalance-reactive tactical traders is a non-trivial custom simulation component central to reproducing market impact results; a naive implementation could be too slow for 128-parallel-env training or could subtly diverge from the paper's dynamics.
risk_assessment.source: SIR architecture modules: LimitOrderBookSimulator, NoiseTraderAgents, TacticalTraderAgents
risk_assessment.mitigation: Implement order book with per-price-level deque-based FIFO queues and vectorize across environments using multiprocessing.Pool (128 workers) matching the paper's reported vectorized architecture; provide a unit test suite (test_order_book.py) checking conservation of shares and correct FIFO matching against hand-constructed scenarios (e.g., reproducing Figure 1's example exactly).

risk_assessment.description: Random seeds are not reported in the paper for exact reproduction of Table 1 numbers.
risk_assessment.mitigation: Document that exact numeric reproduction is not guaranteed; report mean +/- std over multiple seeds in evaluate.py, and reference the paper's own code release (https://github.com/moritzweiss/rlte) as a cross-check in the README.

risk_assessment.description: Training is computationally expensive (paper reports ~1.2-2 hours per configuration on a 64-core/128-thread server); users on modest hardware may not be able to reproduce full results within a reasonable time.
risk_assessment.mitigation: Provide a --quick-test mode in train.py with reduced num_envs/H_iterations for smoke-testing the pipeline, and document expected full-scale runtime and hardware requirements in the README.

risk_assessment.description: GAE lambda=1 vs 0.95 ablation was only tested at a smaller trajectory count (tau=320) in the paper's Appendix C.1, not at the main tau=1280 setting, creating minor ambiguity about the optimal setting for the default config.
risk_assessment.mitigation: Default to lambda=1.0 with tau=1280 (matching Table 1's main results) and expose --gae-lambda and --tau as config-overridable for users who want to replicate the Appendix C.1 ablation.
